mnemonics.title: Smoothing Techniques Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: M-AVE: Moving Averages smooth out the wave - Average out the peaks and valleys to see the true trend direction.

mnemonics.title: Exponential Smoothing Weights
mnemonics.content: ALPHA: As Low (alpha) Puts Higher weight on Ancient data. As High (alpha) Helps Adapt quickly to recent changes.

mnemonics.title: Trend Analysis Steps
mnemonics.content: BASELINE: Build baseline → Analyze patterns → Study seasonality → Identify leading indicators → Look for anomalies → Note external factors → Execute forecast → Evaluate regularly.

mnemonics.title: Forecasting Selection Guide
mnemonics.content: TREND: Trendless data? Use Moving Average. Recent matters most? Use Exponential. Clear direction? Use Linear Regression. Need experts? Use Delphi.

acronyms.usage: Measures average magnitude of forecast errors without considering direction. Lower MAD = more accurate forecast.
Average of squared differences between forecasted and actual values. Penalizes large errors heavily.
Expresses forecast error as a percentage of actual values. Useful for comparing accuracy across different scales.
Square root of MSE. Provides error in same units as the data, easier to interpret than MSE.
Average of last n data points, used to smooth short-term fluctuations and reveal longer-term trends.
Moving average where recent data points get higher weights, more responsive than SMA.
A framework for classifying time series components in exponential smoothing models.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a wave in the ocean. The actual data is choppy water with high peaks and low troughs. Moving Average is like drawing a smooth rope across the wave - it cuts through the noise to show the general direction the water is flowing.

Picture weights on a seesaw. On left: old data on a high platform (low weight). On right: recent data close to pivot (high weight). Alpha determines the distance - higher alpha = recent data sits closer to pivot = more influence.
Think of a magnet pulling scattered points toward a straight rubber band. The rubber band (trend line) finds the best path through the middle of all the points, showing the overall direction.

Draw a sine wave overlaid on a rising line. The sine wave shows the repeating up-down pattern (seasonality), while the underlying rising line shows the overall trend. Together they create a wave pattern.
Think of an S as the growth path: Start flat (slow adoption), then steep middle (rapid growth), finally flatten out at top (market saturation/limited capacity). Classic technology adoption curve.

analogies.analogy: Like a ship's anchor that smooths out the bobbing motion of waves. The ship still moves up and down (actual data), but the anchor line (average) shows the general water level (trend).
Like a heat-seeking missile that tracks the most recent movements of its target. It remembers the whole flight path but focuses most on where the target was just now.
Like a surfer reading ocean waves. The surfer doesn't fight the wave - they read its direction and ride it to their destination. In capacity planning, you read the demand wave and scale accordingly.
Like your resting heart rate. Doctors use it to detect abnormalities. In IT, baseline is your 'normal' resource usage, used to spot when something is wrong or when demand is changing.
Like the smell of rain before a storm arrives. The storm (demand spike) hasn't hit yet, but the smell (indicator) tells you it's coming, giving time to prepare.
Like a car's GPS navigation. As you drive, it continuously recalculates your route based on new information, always showing the next 10 miles - not where you started.
